coredata.dc:title: Moderate microwave-assisted preparation of phthalocyanine-based carbon quantum dots for improved photo-inactivation of bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xiantao
Luo, Zhijin
Zhang, Baoqu
Li, Peiyuan
Xiao, Jun'an
Su, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A pacific synthetic tactic has been used to provide the phthalocyanine-based carbon quantum dots (ZnPc-CQDs) via microwave thermolysis of zinc(II) tetra-amino-phthalocyanine (ZnPc) and citric acid (CA) at 2 min. The ZnPc-CQDs with a granule size of around 5 nm diameter exhibited the prominent stability and water dispersion distinction of carbon quantum dots, as well as the picturesque properties of spectrum. ZnPc-CQDs proved synergistic photothermal outcomes and ROS generation capability, and exhibited potent photoinactivtion for bacteria. ZnPc-CQDs can be anchored to the cell membrane and accumulate in bacterial cells while destroying their integrity. Therefore, ZnPc-CQDs have the potential for PDT/PTT application and provides a new paradigm for antibacterial therapy, which has broad prospects for future biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phthalocyanine
Carbon quantum dots
Antibacterial
PDT
PTT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro bacteria imaging of ZnPc-CQDs
Damaged of ZnPc-CQDs on bacterial cell membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images and DLS measurement of ZnPc-CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) Zeta potentials of ZnPc, ZnPc-CQDs and CQDs. (e) UV–vis absorption and (f) Fluorescence spectra of ZnPc, ZnPc-CQDs in DMF or water (1 × 10−5 mol/L).

(a) Temperature curves of CQDs, ZnPc and ZnPc-CQDs (10 mg/mL) in water of 12 min under 808 nm laser irradiation. (b) Temperature curves of different concentration of ZnPc-CQDs for 12 min under 808 nm laser irradiation. (c) The temperature change of ZnPc-CQDs (10 mg/mL) solution during five cycles of laser on/off. (d) Photothermal effect of ZnPc-CQDs upon 808 nm laser irradiation; inset shows the linear nature of a plot of time versus − ln(θ) seen during the cooling process. (e) The 1O2 formation of CQDs, ZnPc and ZnPc-CQDs in water under 660 nm laser irradiation was determined by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of DPBF at 416 nm. (f) Change of DPBF UV − vis absorption spectrum when ZnPc-CQDs was added.

(a) Agar plate photography of ZnPc and ZnPc-CQDs treated E. coli, respectively, under dark and 660 + 808 nm light-irradiated conditions. The corresponding relationship between bacterial survival and (b) ZnPc, (c) ZnPc-CQDs concentration was determined.
60 + 808 nm light irradiated conditions, respectively. The corresponding relationship between bacterial survival and (b) ZnPc, (c) ZnPc-CQDs concentration was determined.
Confocal images of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus with ZnPc-CQDs (10 mM) under dark and 660 + 808 nm light irradiation conditions.
ROS generation of ZnPc-CQDs (3 μM) in (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus.
(a) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus treatment with ZnPc-CQDs. Zeta potentials of (b) E. coli and (c) S.aureus cells treatment with ZnPc-CQDs.
Synthetic routes of ZnPc-CQDs with PDT/PTT effect.

head.ce:title: Moderate microwave-assisted preparation of phthalocyanine-based carbon quantum dots for improved photo-inactivation of bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pacific synthetic tactic has been used to provide the phthalocyanine-based carbon quantum dots (ZnPc-CQDs) via microwave thermolysis of zinc(II) tetra-amino-phthalocyanine (ZnPc) and citric acid (CA) at 2 min. The ZnPc-CQDs with a granule size of around 5 nm diameter exhibited the prominent stability and water dispersion distinction of carbon quantum dots, as well as the picturesque properties of spectrum. ZnPc-CQDs proved synergistic photothermal outcomes and ROS generation capability, and exhibited potent photoinactivtion for bacteria. ZnPc-CQDs can be anchored to the cell membrane and accumulate in bacterial cells while destroying their integrity. Therefore, ZnPc-CQDs have the potential for PDT/PTT application and provides a new paradigm for antibacterial therapy, which has broad prospects for future biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections present a great challenge to global public health, especially as bacterial resistance to antibiotics increases [1,2], as a result, alternative antibiotic strategies need to be developed. Among the effective alternatives, phototherapy, contains photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) has received exclusive attention due to its heightened non-talerance, non-invasive formations, and localized treatment [3].

In PDT, photosensitizers (PSs) react with surrounding oxygen molecules under light irradiation to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen (1O2), thereby achieving effective treatment [4,5]. Over the past decades, miscellaneous types of PS have been developed and applied [6–8]. Phthalocyanine is a promising PS due to its biocompatibility, better photoactivity and strong absorption in near-infrared (NIR) region (650–850 nm) [9,10]. Currently, although there have been a lot of reports of phthalocyanine for antibacterial treatment of PDT, it may be difficult for a single therapeutic treatment achieve higher therapeutic efficacy in some cases. Therefore, the development of PDT combined with other therapies has attracted great attention [11–13].

In PTT, photothermal nanoagents kill pathogenic bacteria by generating heat under NIR light irradiation [14]. Generally, the temperature range of 50 to 60 °C has been proved to be an appropriate temperature window, where tissue cells are more tolerant in a short time and their bactericidal performance is not compromised [15–20]. Since both PDT and PTT are triggered by near-infrared light, combining them in one system is expected to achieve better therapeutic results [21]. It is one of the common strategies to making PSs [22–24] coated on photothermal agents [25–27].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as good candidates for building a multifunctional phototherapy platform due to their simple preparation, good stability, and potential photochemical and photophysical properties [28,29]. However, the mostly reported absorption/emission of CQDs is located in the visible region currently, which limits the use of CQDs in theranostic [30]. Phthalocyanine-based molecules are widely used as PSs due to their high ROS yield and near infrared spectrum, which has a long light wavelength and good penetration to tissues [31]. Although phthalocyanine is very suitable for PDT, its molecular solubility is poor, and the molecules are easy to aggregate, which hinders the release of ROS and limits the application of PDT [32]. In recent years, supramolecular self-assembled carbon nanodots containing phthalocyanines have been considered as a novel fluorescent agent for biomedical applications due to their high tability and special photochemical properties [33,34]. However, due to the diversity of CQDs and the complexity of living organism, the study of carbon nanodots is still needed to be further studied [35,36].

In this study, a facile one-pot approach to developed phthalocyanine-based carbon quantum dots (ZnPc-CQDs) is put forward with microwave pyrolysis of zinc(II) tetra-amino-phthalocyanine (ZnPc) and citric acid (CA). The ZnPc-CQDs with the advantage of phthalocyanine and carbon quantum dots, such as a good singlet oxygen generation efficiency at 660 nm and a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η) at 808 nm. ZnPc-CQDs showed significantly bacterial photo-inactivation efficiency with PTT and PDT dual effect. ZnPc-CQDs possessed the advantages of (1) well-defined structures come from ZnPc and CA, (2) excellent bacterial photo-inactivation efficiency, and (3) simple preparation through microwave pyrolysis and water. Thus, the ZnPc-CQDs were applied as excellent nanoagents for photothermal/photodynamic synergistic bacterial therapy.

ζ potential has been widely used to characterize charge and the stability of nanomaterials [31]. Compared with CQDs (11.5 mV), the ζ potential of ZnPc-CQDs was 43.5 ± 0.3 mV in water, indicated that ZnPc loading had a significant impact on the surface charge (Fig. 1d). The spectroscopy properties of the ZnPc-CQDs and its precursor ZnPc were also investigated. Compared with free ZnPc in DMF, the Q bands absorption of ZnPc-CQDs (Fig. 1e) in water showed a broadened signal in 670 nm, which suggested the H-aggregation of ZnPc in the nanoparticles [29]. Moreover, at the same concentration, the fluorescence spectra of ZnPc-CQDs in water and DMF and the fluorescence spectra of ZnPc molecules in DMF were determined. As shown in Fig. 1f, ZnPc exhibited a strong emission at 750 nm in DMF, while the emission of ZnPc-CQDs was notably quenched under the same conditions, which was due to the stacking of ZnPc molecules in ZnPc-CQDs. Furthermore, the emission spectra of ZnPc-CQDs were completely quenched in H2O, which may be due to the poor solubility of ZnPc and the strong π − π interaction between molecules, leading to the strong aggregation of molecules. Fluorescence inhibition is beneficial to enhance internal conversion and vibrational relaxation of the nanomaterials in excited states, resulting in higher photothermal conversion efficiency.

The photothermal effects of ZnPc-CQDs was investigated upon irradiation of 808 nm laser. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A, compared with ZnPc and CQDs, the temperature change was 5.5 °C and 10.0 °C, respectively, while the temperature rise of ZnPc-CQDS can reach 34.1 °C (12 min, 808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2). As can be seen from Fig. 2b, the temperature of the ZnPc-CQDs solution increases significantly with the increase of concentration. For example, after irradiation with 0.1 and 10 mg/mL ZnPc-CQDs for 12 min, the temperature increased by 19.4 and 34.1 °C respectively, while the H2O only increased by 5.1 °C. The photothermal cycling test showed that ZnPc-CQDs can maintain the temperature-enhancing curve after multiple times of illumination and cessation of illumination, indicating that ZnPc-CQDs have good photothermal stability (Fig. 2c). Next, the photothermal conversion efficiencies (η) were measured based on the heating/cooling curves. The η of ZnPc-CQDs was calculated to be 27.5%, which was comparable to that of organic small-molecule-based nanoparticles (Fig. 2d) [47,48]. The experimental results showed that ZnPc-CQDs had good PTT effect and possessed the potential to inhibit the invasion of various bacteria [14].

The 1O2 generation in the samples before and after light irradiation was also monitored. Compared with CQDs and the control, the adsorption peak intensity of ZnPc and ZnPc-CQDs decreased obviously with the prolongation of irradiation time, the 1O2 in the two samples (Fig. 2e). After 90 s irradiation, ZnPc-CQDs generates 1O2 at a slightly slower rate than ZnPc. It may be due to some ZnPc being wrapped by CQD, and these results were consistent with the FT-IR spectrum. Furthermore, DPBF as 1O2 indicator was then utilized to detect the 1O2 quantum yield (ΦΔ) of ZnPc-CQDs (Fig. 2f) [45,46,49]. The ΦΔ values of ZnPc-CQDs was calculated to be 3%. Due to the short lifespan of 1O2, the distance traveled is limited [50], after the accumulation of ZnPc-CQDs with this special structure in bacteria, the photothermal effect will cause the photo-triggered release of ZnPc moiety, and the 1O2 will be generated by irradiation, so that the 1O2 will not be ineffective consumed of medium, which is conducive to improve its efficiency. These results indicated that PTT effect of nanoparticles is stronger than PDT effect.

The inhibitory activity of CQDs, ZnPc and ZnPc-CQDs against E. coli were evaluated by use the traditional plate counting method. Bacterial cultures at different concentrations (1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.08 μM) of CQDs, ZnPc, or ZnPc-CQDs in 0.9% NaCl to evaluate their antibacterial effect. In the Fig. 3 a, the number of colonies in the CQDs, ZnPc and control group were roughly the same, suggesting that the treatment of CQDs and ZnPc had no antibacterial effect under dark or light irradiation. In contrast, ZnPc-CQDs exhibited obvious antibacterial effect upon light irradiation.

The statistical results in Fig. 3b showed that ZnPc had no antibacterial effect towards E. coli under light conditions. However, the E. coli viability ofdecreased gradually with the increase of ZnPc-CQD sconcentration under light conditions (Fig. 3c). The bacteria survival rate of ZnPc-CQDs towards E. coli was 48.93%, 42.10% and 22.61% at 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−2 μM, respectively. Especially, the values of survival rates decreased to 2.40% at 0.08 μM.

In order to evaluate the antibacterial universality of ZnPc-CQDs, the inhibition of S. aureus was also performed by the same procedure (Fig. 4 ). The antibacterial activity of ZnPc-CQDs also showed concentration dependence, and the values of bacteria survival rates decreased to 0.50% at 0.08 μM. From the experimental results, it can be concluded that ZnPc-CQDs showed good photoinactivation ability toward both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria upon laser conditions. Moreover, considering that PTT effect of ZnPc-CQDs is stronger than that of PDT effect, PTT may be the key to the antibacterial activity of ZnPc-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bacteria imaging of ZnPc-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The location of PSs is very important for PDT and PTT; hence the fluorescent confocal microscopy was used to monitor the ZnPc-CQDs in bacteria before and after light were treated with drugs. As can be seen from Figs. 5 and 6
, after ZnPc-CQDs treatment, E. coli and S. aureus were labeled with red fluorescence, indicating that ZnPc-CQDs had invaded bacteria and accumulated, thus realizing the fluorescence imaging. These results suggested that ZnPc-CQDs can penetrate cell walls and enter the interior of microorganisms, and finally accumulated in bacterial cells. PDT is considered to be valid and promising tactic for killing bacteria through 1O2 as a highly cytotoxic ROS [37]. However, for the short lifetime and limited migration distance of 1O2 [38], PSs localization is critical for kill efficiency of PDT. ZnPc-CQDs accumulated in the cell by anchoring to the cell membrane of bacteria, allowing the resulting 1O2 produced by ZnPc-CQDs penetrate the cytoplasmic membrane [39] or enter directly into the bacteria [40] and kill them. At the same time, the accumulation of ZnPc-CQDs in bacterial cells also benefited for their PTT effect, because the shorter distances helped to reduce the loss of heat.

The ROS generation of ZnPc-CQDs was further confirmed by DCFH-DA probe in bacteria cells (Fig. 6). Unlike the control group, distinct green emission can be observed in bacteria, which is caused by ROS produced after 660 nm laser irradiation of ZnPc-CQDs [48]. The high reactive oxygen species effect of ZnPc-CQDs makes them ideal materials for antibacterial phototherapy in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Damaged of ZnPc-CQDs on bacterial cell membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of ZnPc-CQDs on bacterial morphology was monitored by SEM (Fig. 7 a). E. coli has smooth and intact surface, after ZnPc-CQDs treatment, abnormal shrinkage and some small film defects appeared on its surface. After irradiation, the cell membrane of E. coli was severely damaged and partially melted. Likewise, pure S. aureus was spherical and had a smooth surface. Under the treatment of ZnPc-CQDs, the membrane of in S. aureus shrinkage slightly. The bacterial membrane became rougher after irradiation, and some membranes were severely damaged. The results intuitively confirmed that ZnPc-CQDs could destroy the integrity of E. coli and S. aureus cell membranes under illumination.

In addition, the charge changes of ZnPc-CQDs were also monitored when they interacted with E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 7b and 7c). E. coli has a highly negative zeta potential (-42.6 ± 0.12 mV), whereas the zeta potential of ZnPc-CQDs was positively charged (43.5 ± 0.30 mV). After incubation of E. coli with ZnPc-CQDs, the zeta potential of E. coli was significantly decreased (-13.1 ± 2.60 mV). Likewise, the negative charge of ZnPC-CQDS treated S. aureus was −13.5 ± 0.80 mV compared to that of S. aureus with a zeta potential of −41.5 ± 0.34 mV. This indicates that ZnPc-CQDs binds to bacterial membranes through electrostatic interaction [41,42], thus destroying its outer membrane and leading to the death of bacteria [43,44].

Wu and coworkers had reports that the phthalocyanine-based nanodots prepared by the hydrothermal method for imaging and PTT anticancer application [32], In this work, we used microwave method to more easily and quickly prepare smaller ZnPc-CQDs (5 nm in diameter), and the ZnPc-CQDs shows photothermal conversion efficiencies (η = 27.5%) and 1O2 generation (ΦΔ = 3.0%). Compared with the single PTT effect of phthalocyanine-based nanodots prepared by the hydrothermal method [32], the ZnPc-CQDs reported here possessed both PDT and PTT abilities, which will provide a strategy for the design of antibacterial system with dual functions of PDT and PTT.

In summary, we developed new phthalocyanine implanted carbon quantum dots (ZnPc-CQDs) by microwave pyrolysis of phthalocyanine and citric acid. ZnPc-CQDs with relatively uniform spherical particles about 5 nm diameter, strong NIR absorption and emission and well water dispersion. ZnPc-CQDs had significant photoinactivation effects of PTT and PDT on both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Confocal and SEM images showed that the synergistic photothermal effect and localization of ROS caused damage to bacterial cell membranes and promoted the cell penetration and accumulation of nanoagents under laser irradiation. As a result, this study provides a simple strategy for designing new generation of antibacterial drugs, which not only improves the efficiency of collaborative photoinactivation, but also not produce antibiotic resistance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51961009), “BAGUI Scholar” Program of Guangxi Province of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109543.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
